proposed_replacement_paragraph: 4. Roaring 40s 500hPa anomaly (W020 / FAIL-004). The dome predicted a 500hPa geopotential height anomaly ≥3% above climatology in the Roaring 40s (40–60°S) latitude band during the test window of March 12–19, 2026. The observed Antarctic Oscillation (AAO) index averaged +0.78σ during the test period — roughly half the ≥+1.5σ threshold that would correspond to a ≥3% anomaly. The prediction failed by a factor of ~2. The dome then retroactively labeled the original prediction "refined," arguing the one-week test window was too narrow for a signal with a 3-month natural timescale. This is a textbook case of moving the goalposts: the dome set the test window, the data came in below threshold, and the dome then declared its own window insufficient. The registry was subsequently changed to require minimum 3-month test windows for atmospheric indices — a rule that did not exist when the prediction was made and that conveniently prevents falsification of the next attempt. If a model's test windows are always adjusted after the fact to exclude failures, the testing framework produces a 100% pass rate by construction.
insertion_point: Replaces the existing FAIL-004 paragraph in SEC-6.5 (part6 in sections.json) which currently lacks quantitative data
kernel_of_truth: There is a legitimate argument that one-week test windows for atmospheric oscillation indices are noisy. The AAO does have multi-month timescales and a single week can be dominated by weather rather than climate signal. But the dome set that window voluntarily, and changing the rules after failure is still goalpost-moving. The correct response would be to acknowledge the failure and make a better-designed prediction with the improved methodology — not to retroactively reclassify the failed prediction as "refined."

numerical_support.source: Dome predictions page (john09289.github.io/predictions), NCEP AAO data
